summary: Joel Spolsky reviews three management books, enthusiastically recommending two and panning the third. He praises Robert Townsend's 1970 classic 'Up the Organization' for its still-relevant advice on corporate management, consultants, and marketing. He equally recommends Michael Lopp's 'Managing Humans' for its insightful take on software engineering management and team dynamics. However, he gently but firmly criticizes 19-year-old Ben Casnocha's 'My Start-Up Life' for being thin on substance and padded with platitudes, while acknowledging the author's intelligence and potential.
key_points: Robert Townsend's 1970 management advice remains surprisingly relevant, including his warnings about conglomerates (now rebranded as big tech acquirers) and management consultants
Marketing should be driven by company leadership through intensive retreats, not delegated to staff departments of 'experts'
Michael Lopp's framework of 'Incrementalists vs. Completionists' captures a fundamental tension in how technical teams approach problem-solving
Youth and intelligence don't substitute for experience when writing about business management — platitudes that feel profound at 19 become embarrassing with hindsight
Self-awareness about one's own writing is a lifelong struggle, even for established writers in their 40s
money_quotes: Conglomerate is just an old word for what you call Yahoo, Microsoft, and Google.
They are people who borrow your watch to tell you what time it is and then walk off with it.
He's the quintessential Silicon Valley middle manager. I hope he doesn't find that term insulting: he's probably the best Silicon Valley middle manager there is.
He's the Doogie Howser, MD of software startups, except for the fact that he probably has no idea who Doogie Howser is, given that the show went off the air when he was 4 years old.
His book, unfortunately, tells you almost nothing about starting a company. It's really, really thin on stories of what the actual company did and how things worked.
You're going to reach the ripe old age of 23, and you're going to look back on this book you wrote, and you're going to say, how on earth did anyone let me publish such self-important crap.
I'm in my 40s, and I'm still morbidly embarrassed by the pompous, arrogant, self-important crap I write on this site here, up to and including this very sentence.
key_insight: The best management wisdom is timeless and experience-tested, which is why a 1970 corporate management book can still outshine a young entrepreneur's freshly published startup memoir.
